## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this section, I'm going to take you through the KPIs and the key things that I am looking out for when I'm analyzing the account. Also how to set up your ads manager to keep an eye on the key metrics. One of the key things that you want to be looking out for is focusing on what the main metrics are that you're looking at, what kind of trends, trend analysis you're looking at as well, and when to take action and when not. First of all, let me take you through ads manager and what I do when I'm picking out the key metrics and why I set those up as well. What I'll do within this is to actually go ahead and create a new custom report. I'm sure, I'm hoping you've already done this and you know how to do this. If not, let me show you how to do it as well as the key metrics that I'm selecting as well. Facebook by default in ads manager allows you to have custom columns set up. I've got one here called main KPIs. There are some built-in ones as well. Some of them are useful for quick analysis, but I prefer to set my own columns up so that I can deep dive into performance. If I go ahead into customize columns. I'll just quickly delete all of the default ones. The way I like to set my columns up, so on the right-hand side here, it'll be set top to bottom, which is then going to translate to left to right, is to place my key metrics on the left-hand side that I want to be monitoring on a daily basis. There's some other metrics which I will also include, which I'm not looking at so frequently, but also what I do is to put all of the key metrics I want into one set of columns and then whether I'm looking at campaigns or ads, those metrics are there for me as well. The first one I'm looking at is amounts spent. I then also want to be looking at my budget as well. The third one I'm looking at is website purchases. Let's pull that one up. I then want to be looking at my cost per website to purchase. Get the right columns up, top of the purchase, cost per website. I also want to be looking at ROAS. They are really the key metrics that I am looking at in AdS Manager. At this stage, I then break it out into my funnels. These are the top line KPIs. The funnel starts with impressions, goes down to clicks, and then goes deeper into your

## [03:01 - 06:01]

funnel and your tracked parts of that funnel. Let me explain. Here I'm going to type in impressions because that's the key thing that I want to be looking at at top level alongside frequency. Difference between reach and impressions. Reach is how many people you're reaching and impressions is how many times the ads were served. The difference being that one person can have two impressions, for example. That's why I prefer to have impressions and then frequency. Impressions is how many times my ad was served. How many times that has been served to one individual person. Next thing I'm looking at is my click-through rate. With the click-through rate, you have three different options here. It's really interesting to know what the differences are and when to use them or perhaps when not to use them. The link click on an ad is any click either on the ad. You can hit in the read more when you've got long form copy or clicking out into your website for example. Link clicks will include all of that. Outbound clicks are off of Facebook. For example, if you want to track how many people are clicking an ad and actually visiting your website, then you're probably going to track outbound clicks. CTRL is basically any kind of click on an ad, off of Facebook, on Facebook. It includes a whole range of other things that you're probably not of interest when you're looking at Econ performance. Depending on the type of ads you're using, the link click-through rate is, or at least tracking the links that are coming through from link clicks. Can be useful for example if you're running Canvas ads for example and you want to track people that are interacting with the ad and actually open up the Canvas so you can look at link clicks and then look at outbound clicks to see how many actually then go off to visit your website for example. I'm going to include both outbound CTRL and the link CTRL as well. Another measure I'm looking at is CPM. The cost per thousand impressions, it's one of the key metrics of determining how well you are targeting, how well you're performing the auction. You want it as low as possible and also the type of objective plays a big part in here. The CPMs that you'll get through video and page post engagement will be way, way lower than website conversion, especially when you're going to really competitive niches as well. It's a good metric to keep an eye on. The auction is driven off CPMs so you'll know how well you're performing there. Cost per click is the next one. I want to be looking at the cost I am paying per click. At the moment we have only the option for cost per link click, not the cost per outbound click. This is a calculated metric based on your cost per thousand impressions.

## [06:01 - 09:02]

Unlike Google AdWords, which is all driven off of the cost that you're paying for every individual click, with Facebook you're paying on impressions and your reach and then there's a calculated number that comes back. This is still a good metric for us to keep an eye on as well. Then I want to track the absolute numbers. Link click, now bound clicks to see how well my ads and campaigns are performing. The other one that I would also be looking at is also looking at the landing page reviews. This is more of a recent addition and it allows you to look at how many people are actually clicking your ad and how many are firing the landing page event back to Facebook as well to see how many people are accessing. Maybe some spam links are going on or maybe some page loading issues. For example, the next one I'm looking at and a typical part of an e-commerce setup is content views as well. The way most e-commerce sites are set up is that you have your home page, your category page, your product page, your ad to cart event, your cart page and then all the way through the checkout. It's good practice to put your key pages under website content views. You can use a custom event if you want, but the most typical way of setting up is generally your product pages would have the website content views event attached and maybe other key events as well. That's the next level down on my funnel. Then after that I want to have a look at how many people are adding to cart. If I go to ads to cart. If you go back down here, I'm now building out my funnel so I can track the absolute metrics here all the way down to ads to cart. Then if it's installed, the next... So in fact, actually after you do add to cart, some e-commerce sites will also have a cart page event as well. This is not a standard event as per, for example, Shopify, but it's something which I prefer to have installed as well. For this particular example, I've created a custom conversion event for the view cart page event and I've made it part of the ad to cart events as well. So I'm going to add that into this. The next one is to initiate the payment. So let's have a look at website checkout's initiated and the payment info is then the final step. Let's type that in. And then of course we have our purchase pixel which we've already added further up. So at this point that kind of completes my funnel up to the point of checkout.

## [09:02 - 12:02]

So that's my kind of next layer down of reporting. However, because as I mentioned before, I prefer my ads manager to have all the kind of columns in one report. You can split these out into multiple reports and save them as different options in your drop down, but I prefer to have them all. The next thing that I'm looking at is my delivery insights. So we'll cover delivery insights in a separate video. Delivery insights is crucial to understanding what's working and what's not at your ad set level, your reach within an ad set, your overlapping, etc. Then I'm also generally including my video view metrics. So if you're not running video free commerce, you're missing out on a huge opportunity. Generally I would always recommend to be running videos of your product, of maybe some brand or aspirational shots or even testimonials. But this is where I now want to see how my videos are performing and see how far people are getting as well. I'll be tracking the 25 up to 95% generally, if the hit 95% most will hit 100. I'm also looking at the 10 second video views metrics. So three seconds, people may accidentally watch it. 30 seconds, your video is probably not that long, but 10 seconds is a good measure. One thing to also bear in mind is when people skip parts of the video and let's say they get to the 75% mark, they may have actually skipped part of it as well. So actually 10 seconds is a different way of looking at engagement for your video as well. Then I'm looking at my ad level metrics. So the way the reporting setup works in ads manager is if these metrics don't apply at that level, they won't show. So for example, I want to be looking at my relevant score. It won't show the campaign and ad set level. But then if I drill down to the ad level, all of a sudden it will pop up. So if I'm analyzing and I'm drilling down, I've got my column set, then I know that relevant score will appear. So I can start using that as well. Alongside relevant score, you have to be looking at positive and negative feedback. Positive feedback is that people are clicking, engaging, not to visit your site and bounce in straight back. So all the positive kind of actions are related to an ad. Negative feedback is absolutely the one you want to avoid. Negative feedback is the majority of it's driven by people explicitly saying they don't want to see your ads anymore. And that has a big negative factor on your ad and also your ad set performance. So you absolutely want to keep an eye on this and keep this as low as possible. If you are starting to see high negative feedback, it's really, you know, people to look at frequency, relevant score, etc. For me, if I'm starting to see negative feedback on an ad, that's a stronger indicator that I'd probably need to action something here as well. So that's something which you might want to bear in mind as you're doing your analysis as well.
